preFix.title: Goose fat roast potatoes
preFix.subtitle: The Christmas-day standard
preFix.excerpt: Floury potatoes par-boiled, shaken rough, and roasted in goose fat until shattering crisp. The richer, more golden version of the classic Sunday roast potato.
preFix.sourceNotes: Technique adapted from standard British roast potato method. Goose fat became widely available in UK supermarkets in the 1990s and is now associated with Christmas roasting.

content.text: Goose fat roast potatoes are the Christmas version of the Sunday classic. The fat gives a richer, more golden crust than duck fat and a faint savouriness that pairs well with turkey and goose. The method is identical to the standard duck fat version; the fat is the only change.

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: Maris Piper or King Edward, peeled and cut into large even chunks

items.name: Duck fat
items.prepNote: goose fat (duck fat is an acceptable substitute)

content.text: Cover the potato chunks with cold salted water in a large saucepan. Bring to the boil and
for 8 to 10 minutes, until the edges are just softening but the centres are still firm.
Drain well. Shake the colander firmly several times to rough up and fluff the surface of each piece. Leave to steam-dry for 5 minutes.
Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Put the goose fat into a wide roasting tin and heat for 10 minutes until the fat is shimmering and beginning to smoke.
Carefully add the potatoes and turn them in the fat. Add the rosemary and garlic if using. Season with the salt.
Roast for 45 to 50 minutes, turning once or twice, until deeply golden and crisp on all sides.

items.fix: Heat the fat until smoking. Space the potatoes with a little room between each; use two tins if needed.
items.cause: The fat was not hot enough, or the tin was overcrowded.
items.symptom: Potatoes are golden but not really crisp

items.fix: Leave for at least 25 minutes before the first turn. They release naturally once a crust has formed underneath.
items.cause: Turned before the crust has set.
items.symptom: Potatoes are sticking to the tin

items.fix: Cut the potatoes to similar sizes. Rotate the tin halfway through roasting.
items.cause: Uneven potato sizes, or the tin has hot spots.
items.symptom: Uneven browning: some very dark, others pale

items.fix: Par-boil only until the edges soften, not until the centres are soft.
items.cause: Over-par-boiled so the flesh cooked through before roasting.
items.symptom: Dense, floury rather than fluffy inside

content.text: Goose fat has been used in British cooking since the medieval period, but it became a mainstream supermarket product in the early 1990s when Marks and Spencer started selling it in tins. It became synonymous with Christmas roasties through a combination of good marketing and genuine quality. The fat renders from a Christmas goose that many households no longer roast, but the tin in the fridge has become its own tradition.
